Understanding Async Rust

Marcus Wei January 12, 2024 8 min read

Rust's async/await syntax looks familiar if you've used JavaScript or Python, but the underlying model is fundamentally different. Understanding these differences is key to writing efficient async Rust code.

Futures Are Lazy

In JavaScript, calling an async function immediately starts execution. In Rust, creating a future does nothing until you .await it or hand it to an executor. This is the lazy evaluation model.

async fn fetch_data() -> Result<Data, Error> {
    // This doesn't run until awaited
    let response = client.get(url).send().await?;
    response.json().await
}

Executors Are External

Rust doesn't ship with a built-in async runtime. You choose your executor: Tokio, async-std, smol, or even write your own. This gives you control over scheduling, but means you need to make a choice.

Pin and Unpin

Async functions in Rust are compiled to state machines that may contain self-references. The Pin type ensures these self-referential futures can't be moved in memory, which would invalidate internal pointers.

Most of the time, you don't need to think about Pin directly — .await handles it for you. But when writing combinators or manual futures, it becomes important.

Conclusion

Async Rust is powerful but has a steeper learning curve than other languages. The payoff is zero-cost abstractions and predictable performance without garbage collection pauses.
